title: 1. Variables & Basic Types
description:
A variable is a named container that stores a value. In Python, you don't need to declare the type of a variable — Python figures it out automatically. This is called dynamic typing.

Core Data Types

  • int — Whole numbers (e.g., 10, -5)
  • float — Decimal numbers (e.g., 3.14, -0.5)
  • str — Text, enclosed in single or double quotes (e.g., "Python")
  • bool — Logical values: True or False

You can check the type of any variable using type(variable), and convert between types using int(), float(), str(), and bool().
code:
# Integer, Float, String, Boolean
x = 10
y = 3.14
name = "Python"
is_active = True

# Check types
print(type(x))        # <class 'int'>
print(type(name))     # <class 'str'>

# Type conversion
str_x = str(x)        # '10'
int_y = int(y)        # 3
float_x = float(x)   # 10.0

title: 2. Math Operations
description:
Python supports all standard mathematical operations. Arithmetic operators work on numbers and can be combined with assignment using shorthand operators like +=, -=, etc.

Operator Precedence

Python follows the standard BODMAS/PEMDAS order: Parentheses → Exponents → Multiplication/Division → Addition/Subtraction. Use parentheses to control the order explicitly.

Key Operators

  • // — Floor division: divides and rounds down to the nearest integer
  • % — Modulo: returns the remainder of a division. Very useful for checking even/odd numbers
  • ** — Exponentiation: raises a number to a power
code:
a = 10
b = 3

add  = a + b      # 13
sub  = a - b      # 7
mul  = a * b      # 30
div  = a / b      # 3.333...
floor = a // b   # 3  (Floor division)
mod  = a % b      # 1  (Modulo / Remainder)
exp  = a ** b     # 1000 (Exponentiation)

# Shorthand operators
a += 5   # a = a + 5 -> 15
a *= 2   # a = a * 2 -> 30

# Check if a number is even
print(10 % 2 == 0)  # True

title: 3. Strings & Formatting
description:
Strings (str) are sequences of characters. They are immutable, meaning once created, their content cannot be changed in-place — any operation returns a new string.

f-Strings (Recommended)

f-Strings (formatted string literals) are the modern, preferred way to embed variables and expressions directly inside a string. Prefix the string with f and wrap expressions in {}.

Essential String Methods

  • .upper() / .lower() — Change case
  • .strip() — Remove leading/trailing whitespace
  • .split(sep) — Split into a list by a separator
  • .join(list) — Join a list of strings into one
  • .replace(old, new) — Replace substrings
  • len(s) — Get the number of characters
code:
name = "Alice"
age = 25

# f-Strings (Python 3.6+)
msg = f"{name} is {age} years old."
print(msg)  # Alice is 25 years old.

# Expressions inside f-strings
print(f"Next year she'll be {age + 1}")

# String Methods
print("hello".capitalize())   # 'Hello'
print("hello".upper())        # 'HELLO'
print("  hello  ".strip())    # 'hello'
print("a,b,c".split(","))     # ['a', 'b', 'c']
print("-".join(['a', 'b']))   # 'a-b'
print("hello".replace("l","r"))  # 'herro'
print(len("hello"))           # 5

# Multiline strings
poem = """
Roses are red,
Violets are blue.
"""

title: 4. Lists (Mutable)
description:
A list is an ordered, mutable (changeable) collection of items. Lists can store any mix of data types, including other lists. They are one of the most commonly used data structures in Python.

Indexing

Lists are zero-indexed: the first item is at index 0. Negative indices count from the end: -1 is the last item.

Slicing

You can extract a portion of a list using list[start:end:step]. The end index is exclusive.

Common List Methods

  • .append(x) — Add to the end
  • .insert(i, x) — Insert at a specific index
  • .pop() — Remove and return the last item
  • .remove(x) — Remove the first occurrence of a value
  • .sort() — Sort in-place
  • .reverse() — Reverse in-place
code:
nums = [1, 2, 3]

# Accessing items
print(nums[0])    # 1 (first)
print(nums[-1])   # 3 (last)

# Modifying
nums.append(4)       # [1, 2, 3, 4]
nums.insert(0, 0)    # [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
nums.pop()           # Removes last: 4
nums.remove(2)       # Removes first '2'
nums.sort()          # Sorts in-place

# Slicing [start:end:step]
print(nums[1:3])     # Items at index 1, 2
print(nums[::-1])    # Reversed list

# Useful functions
print(len(nums))     # Number of items
print(sum(nums))     # Sum of all items
print(min(nums), max(nums))

title: 5. Tuples (Immutable)
description:
A tuple is an ordered, immutable (unchangeable) collection. Once created, you cannot add, remove, or change its items. Tuples are faster than lists and are used when data should not be modified.

When to Use Tuples

  • Representing fixed data like coordinates (x, y), RGB colors (r, g, b), or database records
  • As dictionary keys (lists cannot be used as keys because they are mutable)
  • Returning multiple values from a function

Unpacking

You can assign all elements of a tuple to individual variables in a single line — this is called tuple unpacking.
code:
# Creating a tuple
coords = (10, 20)
rgb = (255, 128, 0)

# Accessing elements
x = coords[0]   # 10

# Unpacking
x, y = coords
print(x, y)  # 10 20

# Tuples are immutable - this would raise an error:
# coords[0] = 15  -> TypeError!

# Tuple with one element needs a trailing comma
singleton = (42,)

# Named tuple for clarity
from collections import namedtuple
Point = namedtuple('Point', ['x', 'y'])
p = Point(3, 4)
print(p.x, p.y)  # 3 4

title: 6. Dictionaries
description:
A dictionary (dict) is an unordered collection of key-value pairs. Keys must be unique and immutable (strings, numbers, or tuples). Dictionaries are extremely fast for lookups.

When to Use Dicts

Use dictionaries when you need to associate a piece of data with a name/key, such as storing user profiles, configuration settings, or counting occurrences of items.

Key Methods

  • .get(key, default) — Safe access; returns default if key doesn't exist
  • .keys() — All keys
  • .values() — All values
  • .items() — All key-value pairs (as tuples)
  • .pop(key) — Remove and return value for key
  • .update(other_dict) — Merge another dict in
code:
# Creating
user = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25}

# Accessing
print(user["name"])         # 'Alice'
print(user.get("job", "N/A")) # 'N/A' (safe, no error)

# Adding / Updating
user["city"] = "New York"   # Add new key
user["age"] = 26            # Update existing key

# Deleting
del user["city"]

# Looping
for key, val in user.items():
    print(f"{key}: {val}")

# Dictionary from two lists
keys = ["a", "b", "c"]
vals = [1, 2, 3]
d = dict(zip(keys, vals))   # {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}

title: 7. Sets (Unique)
description:
A set is an unordered collection of unique elements. Duplicate values are automatically removed. Sets are very efficient for membership tests (in operator) and set algebra.

Key Characteristics

  • No duplicate values
  • Unordered — no indexing
  • Very fast for in checks (faster than lists)

Set Operations

  • | or .union() — All elements from both sets
  • & or .intersection() — Only elements in both sets
  • - or .difference() — Elements in one set but not the other
  • ^ or .symmetric_difference() — Elements in either set, but not both

title: 8. Conditionals
description:
Conditional statements allow your program to make decisions. Python uses if, elif (else if), and else to branch logic based on whether conditions are True or False.

Comparison Operators

  • == equal to | != not equal
  • > greater | < less | >= / <= greater/less or equal

Logical Operators

  • and — Both conditions must be True
  • or — At least one must be True
  • not — Inverts the condition

Truthy & Falsy Values

In Python, the following values are considered Falsy: 0, "" (empty string), [], {}, None. Everything else is Truthy.

title: 9. Loops
description:
Loops repeat a block of code multiple times. Python has two types of loops: for and while.

for Loop

Iterates over any iterable object — a list, string, range, dictionary, etc. The range(start, stop, step) function generates a sequence of numbers.

while Loop

Repeats as long as a condition is True. Be careful to ensure the condition eventually becomes False, or the loop will run forever.

Loop Control

  • break — Immediately exit the loop
  • continue — Skip the rest of this iteration and go to the next
  • pass — Do nothing (placeholder)

Useful Built-ins for Loops

  • enumerate() — Get both index and value
  • zip() — Loop over two lists simultaneously
code:
# For loop over a range
for i in range(5):
    print(i)  # 0, 1, 2, 3, 4

# For loop over a list
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

# While loop
count = 0
while count < 3:
    print(count)  # 0, 1, 2
    count += 1

# Loop control
for i in range(10):
    if i == 3: continue   # Skip 3
    if i == 6: break      # Stop at 6
    print(i)

# Enumerate (get index + value)
for idx, val in enumerate(["A", "B", "C"]):
    print(idx, val)   # 0 A, 1 B, 2 C

# Zip (loop two lists together)
names = ["Alice", "Bob"]
scores = [90, 85]
for n, s in zip(names, scores):
    print(f"{n}: {s}")

title: 10. Functions
description:
A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. Functions help you organize code, reduce repetition (DRY principle), and make programs easier to read and test.

Defining a Function

Use the def keyword, followed by the function name and parameters in parentheses.

Parameters vs Arguments

  • Parameters — Variables listed in the function definition
  • Arguments — Actual values passed when calling the function

Default Parameters

You can give parameters a default value, making them optional when calling the function.

Return Values

Use return to send a result back to the caller. A function without a return statement returns None.
code:
# Basic function
def greet(name, msg="Hello"):
    return f"{msg}, {name}!"

print(greet("Alice"))           # Hello, Alice!
print(greet("Bob", "Hi"))       # Hi, Bob!

# Multiple return values (returns a tuple)
def min_max(nums):
    return min(nums), max(nums)

lo, hi = min_max([3, 1, 4, 1, 5])
print(lo, hi)  # 1 5

# Type hinting (Python 3.5+)
def add(x: int, y: int) -> int:
    return x + y

# Docstrings
def calculate_area(radius: float) -> float:
    """Calculate the area of a circle.
    
    Args:
        radius: The circle's radius.
    Returns:
        The area as a float.
    """
    import math
    return math.pi * radius ** 2

title: 11. *args & **kwargs
description:
Sometimes you don't know in advance how many arguments a function needs. Python provides two special syntaxes for this:

*args (Variable Positional Arguments)

The *args syntax collects any number of positional arguments into a tuple. The name args is a convention — the * is what matters.

**kwargs (Variable Keyword Arguments)

The **kwargs syntax collects any number of keyword arguments (name=value pairs) into a dictionary. The name kwargs is also a convention.

Order of Parameters

When using both, the order must be: regular params → *args → **kwargs.

Unpacking with * and **

You can also use * and ** when calling a function to unpack a list or dictionary as arguments.

title: 12. Lambda Functions
description:
A lambda is a small, anonymous (unnamed) function defined in a single line. It can take any number of arguments but can only contain a single expression. The result of that expression is automatically returned.

Syntax

lambda arguments: expression

When to Use Lambdas

Lambdas are best used for short, throwaway functions, especially as arguments to higher-order functions like sorted(), map(), and filter(). For anything more complex, use a regular def function for better readability.

map() and filter()

  • map(func, iterable) — Apply a function to every item in a list
  • filter(func, iterable) — Keep only items where the function returns True
code:
# Equivalent regular function and lambda
def add(x, y): return x + y
add_lambda = lambda x, y: x + y

print(add_lambda(5, 3))  # 8

# Used with sorted() - sort by a specific key
users = [{"name": "Bob", "age": 30}, {"name": "Alice", "age": 25}]
users.sort(key=lambda u: u["age"])
print(users[0]["name"])  # Alice (youngest first)

# Used with map() - apply to every item
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
squares = list(map(lambda x: x**2, nums))
print(squares)  # [1, 4, 9, 16]

# Used with filter() - keep only matching items
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums))
print(evens)  # [2, 4]

title: 13. Comprehensions
description:
Comprehensions provide a concise, readable way to create collections (lists, dicts, sets) from existing iterables. They are often faster than an equivalent for loop.

List Comprehension

Creates a new list. Syntax: [expression for item in iterable if condition]

Dictionary Comprehension

Creates a new dict. Syntax: {key: value for item in iterable}

Set Comprehension

Creates a new set (duplicates removed). Syntax: {expression for item in iterable}

Generator Expression

Like a list comprehension but uses () instead of []. It's lazy — it generates items one at a time and is very memory-efficient for large data.
code:
# List Comprehension
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
squares = [x**2 for x in nums]
print(squares)  # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

# With condition (filter)
evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)  # [2, 4]

# Nested comprehension
matrix = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
flat = [n for row in matrix for n in row]
print(flat)  # [1, 2, 3, 4]

# Dictionary Comprehension
square_map = {x: x**2 for x in range(5)}
print(square_map)  # {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9, 4: 16}

# Set Comprehension (unique chars)
chars = {c for c in "hello world"}
print(chars)  # {'h', 'e', 'l', 'o', ' ', 'w', 'r', 'd'}

# Generator expression (memory-efficient)
total = sum(x**2 for x in range(1000000))

title: 14. Classes & OOP
description:
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming paradigm based on the concept of objects — bundles of data (attributes) and behavior (methods). A class is a blueprint for creating objects.

Key OOP Concepts

  • Class — The blueprint (e.g., Dog)
  • Object / Instance — A specific creation from the blueprint (e.g., my_dog)
  • Attribute — Data attached to an object (e.g., name, age)
  • Method — A function defined inside a class

The __init__ Method (Constructor)

This special method is automatically called whenever a new object is created. It initializes the object's attributes.

self

self refers to the specific instance of the class. It must be the first parameter of every instance method.
code:
class Dog:
    # Class Attribute (shared by all instances)
    species = "Canis familiaris"

    # Constructor (__init__)
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name  # Instance Attribute
        self.age = age

    # Instance Method
    def bark(self):
        return f"{self.name} says Woof!"

    def describe(self):
        return f"{self.name} is {self.age} years old."

# Creating objects (instances)
dog1 = Dog("Rex", 3)
dog2 = Dog("Buddy", 5)

print(dog1.bark())        # Rex says Woof!
print(dog2.describe())    # Buddy is 5 years old.
print(Dog.species)        # Canis familiaris

title: 15. Inheritance & Magic Methods
description:
Inheritance allows a new class (child/subclass) to acquire the attributes and methods of an existing class (parent/superclass). This promotes code reuse.

super()

Use super() to call a method from the parent class, most commonly in __init__ to initialize parent attributes.

Polymorphism

A subclass can override a parent's method with its own version. This is called method overriding.

Magic (Dunder) Methods

Methods surrounded by double underscores like __str__, __len__, __add__ are called magic methods or dunder methods. They let you define how your objects behave with built-in Python operators and functions.

  • __str__ — Called by print() and str()
  • __len__ — Called by len()
  • __add__ — Called by the + operator

title: 16. Exception Handling
description:
Errors (exceptions) that occur during program execution can crash your program. Exception handling lets you catch and respond to errors gracefully instead of crashing.

try / except / else / finally

  • try — Code that might raise an error
  • except — What to do if a specific error occurs
  • else — Runs only if the try block succeeded (no exception)
  • finally — Always runs (cleanup code like closing files)

Common Built-in Exceptions

  • ValueError — Wrong value type (e.g., int("abc"))
  • TypeError — Wrong type used
  • IndexError — List index out of range
  • KeyError — Dictionary key not found
  • ZeroDivisionError — Dividing by zero
  • FileNotFoundError — File does not exist

title: 17. File Handling
description:
Python makes it easy to read from and write to files on your system. Always use a context manager (with statement) when working with files — it automatically closes the file even if an error occurs, preventing resource leaks.

File Modes

  • 'r' — Read (default)
  • 'w' — Write (overwrites existing content)
  • 'a' — Append (adds to end without overwriting)
  • 'rb' / 'wb' — Binary read/write (for images, PDFs, etc.)

Reading Methods

  • .read() — Read entire file as a single string
  • .readline() — Read one line
  • .readlines() — Read all lines into a list
  • Iterating with a for loop — Most memory-efficient for large files
code:
# Writing to a file (with context manager)
with open("data.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello Python\n")
    f.write("Second line\n")

# Reading entire file at once
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()
print(content)

# Reading line by line (memory-efficient)
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())  # strip removes \n

# Reading into a list of lines
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()  # ['Hello Python\n', ...]

# Appending
with open("data.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write("Appended line\n")

# Check if file exists before reading
import os
if os.path.exists("data.txt"):
    with open("data.txt") as f:
        print(f.read())

title: 18. Scope & Global
description:
Scope determines where a variable is accessible. Python uses the LEGB rule to resolve variable names, searching in this order:

  1. Local — Inside the current function
  2. Enclosing — In the enclosing function (for nested functions)
  3. Global — At the top level of the script/module
  4. Built-in — Python's built-in names like print, len

global keyword

Use global x inside a function to explicitly read and modify a global variable. Generally, overusing globals is considered bad practice; prefer passing values as arguments and returning results.

nonlocal keyword

Use nonlocal x inside a nested function to modify a variable from the enclosing (not global) scope.
code:
x = 10  # Global variable

def read_global():
    print(x)  # Can read globals without 'global'

def modify_global():
    global x
    x = 20   # Modifies the global x

read_global()   # 10
modify_global()
print(x)        # 20

# Nonlocal in nested functions
def outer():
    count = 0
    def inner():
        nonlocal count   # Refers to outer's 'count'
        count += 1
    inner()
    inner()
    print(count)  # 2

outer()

# Local shadows global
y = 100
def shadow():
    y = 999   # Creates a new LOCAL y
    print(y)  # 999
shadow()
print(y)  # 100 - global unchanged

title: 19. Iterators & Generators
description:
An iterator is any object that returns its elements one at a time. Python's for loop uses iterators under the hood. You can manually iterate with next().

A generator is a special function that produces a sequence of values lazily, one at a time, using the yield keyword. It pauses execution and resumes from where it left off on the next call.

Why Use Generators?

  • They generate values on-demand, so they use almost no memory
  • Perfect for large datasets (e.g., reading a huge file line by line)
  • They can represent infinite sequences

Generator vs List

A list stores all values in memory at once. A generator computes each value when needed — this makes it much more memory-efficient.
code:
# Generator function
def count_up_to(n):
    count = 1
    while count <= n:
        yield count   # Pause and return value
        count += 1

counter = count_up_to(3)
print(next(counter))  # 1
print(next(counter))  # 2
print(next(counter))  # 3

# Use in a for loop
for num in count_up_to(5):
    print(num)  # 1 2 3 4 5

# Infinite generator
def fibonacci():
    a, b = 0, 1
    while True:
        yield a
        a, b = b, a + b

fib = fibonacci()
for _ in range(8):
    print(next(fib), end=" ")
# 0 1 1 2 3 5 8 13

# Generator expression (vs list comprehension)
# List: stores ALL values in memory
big_list = [x**2 for x in range(1_000_000)]
# Generator: computes ONE at a time
big_gen = (x**2 for x in range(1_000_000))

title: 20. Decorators
description:
A decorator is a function that wraps another function to extend or modify its behavior — without permanently changing the original function. They use the @decorator_name syntax.

How They Work

When you write @my_decorator above a function func, Python is essentially doing: func = my_decorator(func). The decorator takes the original function, wraps it inside another function, and returns the wrapper.

functools.wraps

Always use @functools.wraps(func) inside your decorator to preserve the original function's name and docstring. Without it, debugging becomes harder.

Common Real-World Uses

  • Timing / profiling functions
  • Logging function calls
  • Input validation and caching
  • Flask's @app.route is a decorator!

title: 21. Modules & Imports
description:
A module is a Python file containing code (functions, classes, variables) that can be reused across other files. Python has a large Standard Library of built-in modules, plus a huge ecosystem of third-party packages installable via pip.

Import Styles

  • import module — Import the whole module, access with module.item
  • from module import item — Import a specific item directly
  • import module as alias — Give the module a shorter alias

Useful Standard Library Modules

  • os — File system and OS operations
  • sys — System-specific parameters
  • math — Math functions
  • random — Random number generation
  • datetime — Date and time handling
  • json — JSON encoding/decoding

Creating Your Own Module

Any .py file is a module. Create utils.py and import utils from another file.
code:
# Import the whole module
import math
print(math.pi)        # 3.14159...
print(math.sqrt(16))  # 4.0
print(math.ceil(4.3)) # 5

# Import specific items
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
now = datetime.now()
print(now.strftime("%Y-%m-%d"))

# Import with alias
import random as rnd
print(rnd.randint(1, 100))   # Random int 1-100
print(rnd.choice(["a","b","c"]))

# Import everything (avoid in large projects)
from math import *
print(sqrt(25))  # 5.0

# os module
import os
print(os.getcwd())           # Current directory
files = os.listdir('.')      # List files in dir
os.makedirs('new_dir', exist_ok=True)  # Create dir

title: 22. JSON Handling
description:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, text-based format for storing and exchanging data. Python's built-in json module makes it easy to work with JSON.

Key Functions

  • json.dumps(obj) — Convert Python object → JSON string (Dump to String)
  • json.loads(s) — Convert JSON string → Python object (Load from String)
  • json.dump(obj, file) — Write Python object to a JSON file
  • json.load(file) — Read JSON from a file → Python object

Python ↔ JSON Type Mapping

  • dict ↔ JSON object {}
  • list ↔ JSON array []
  • str ↔ JSON string
  • int/float ↔ JSON number
  • True/False ↔ true/false
  • None ↔ null
code:
import json

data = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25, "active": True}

# Python dict -> JSON string
json_str = json.dumps(data)
print(json_str)  # '{"name": "Alice", "age": 25, ...}'
print(type(json_str))  # <class 'str'>

# Pretty-print with indentation
pretty = json.dumps(data, indent=2)
print(pretty)

# JSON string -> Python dict
parsed = json.loads(json_str)
print(parsed["name"])  # Alice

# Write JSON to a file
with open('data.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=2)

# Read JSON from a file
with open('data.json', 'r') as f:
    loaded = json.load(f)
print(loaded)  # {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25, ...}

title: 23. Collections Module
description:
The collections module provides specialized container datatypes that extend Python's built-in list, dict, and tuple.

Counter

Counts the occurrences of elements in a sequence. Returns a dict-like object where keys are elements and values are their counts.

defaultdict

A dictionary that automatically creates a default value for missing keys, preventing KeyError.

deque (double-ended queue)

A list-like object optimized for fast append and pop from both ends. Ideal for implementing queues and stacks.

OrderedDict

A dict that remembers insertion order. (In Python 3.7+, regular dicts also preserve order, so this is less needed now.)
code:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict, deque

# Counter: count occurrences
words = ["apple", "banana", "apple", "cherry", "banana", "apple"]
counts = Counter(words)
print(counts)               # Counter({'apple': 3, 'banana': 2, 'cherry': 1})
print(counts["apple"])      # 3
print(counts.most_common(2)) # [('apple', 3), ('banana', 2)]

# defaultdict: avoid KeyError
from collections import defaultdict
graph = defaultdict(list)   # Default value is an empty list
graph["A"].append("B")      # No KeyError!
graph["A"].append("C")
print(dict(graph))           # {'A': ['B', 'C']}

# deque: efficient queue / stack
queue = deque([1, 2, 3])
queue.append(4)       # Add to right
queue.appendleft(0)   # Add to left
print(queue.popleft())  # 0 (fast FIFO)

title: 24. Regular Expressions (re)
description:
Regular expressions (regex) are powerful patterns used to search, match, and manipulate text. Python's re module provides full regex support.

Common re Functions

  • re.findall(pattern, text) — Find all matches, return a list
  • re.search(pattern, text) — Find the first match anywhere in the string
  • re.match(pattern, text) — Check if the string starts with the pattern
  • re.sub(pattern, repl, text) — Replace all matches with a replacement
  • re.split(pattern, text) — Split string by a pattern

Common Pattern Elements

  • \d — Any digit | \w — Any word character | \s — Any whitespace
  • . — Any character | ^ — Start | $ — End
  • * — 0+ times | + — 1+ times | ? — 0 or 1 time
  • {n,m} — Between n and m times
code:
import re

text = "Contact alice@email.com or bob@test.org"

# Find all email addresses
emails = re.findall(r'[\w.]+@[\w.]+\.[a-z]{2,}', text)
print(emails)  # ['alice@email.com', 'bob@test.org']

# Search for a pattern
match = re.search(r'\d+', "I have 42 apples")
if match:
    print(match.group())  # '42'

# Substitution (replace)
result = re.sub(r'\d+', 'NUM', "ID: 123, Score: 456")
print(result)  # 'ID: NUM, Score: NUM'

# Validation (does it match?)
phone_pattern = r'^\d{3}-\d{3}-\d{4}$'
print(bool(re.match(phone_pattern, "123-456-7890")))  # True
print(bool(re.match(phone_pattern, "123456789")))     # False

# Split by multiple separators
parts = re.split(r'[,;\s]+', "one, two;  three")
print(parts)  # ['one', 'two', 'three']

title: 25. Datetime Module
description:
The datetime module allows you to work with dates and times. It's part of Python's standard library.

Key Classes

  • datetime.now() — Current date and time
  • datetime.date — Just a date (year, month, day)
  • datetime.time — Just a time (hour, minute, second)
  • timedelta — Represents a duration (difference between two dates)

Formatting

  • .strftime(format) — Convert datetime to a formatted string (String Format Time)
  • datetime.strptime(s, format) — Convert a formatted string to a datetime (String Parse Time)

Common Format Codes

  • %Y — 4-digit year | %m — Month (01-12) | %d — Day (01-31)
  • %H — Hour (00-23) | %M — Minute | %S — Second
code:
from datetime import datetime, date, timedelta

# Current datetime
now = datetime.now()
print(now)  # 2024-01-15 10:30:00.123456

# Format to string
formatted = now.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
print(formatted)  # '2024-01-15 10:30:00'

# Parse string to datetime
dt = datetime.strptime("25-12-2024", "%d-%m-%Y")
print(dt.year, dt.month, dt.day)  # 2024 12 25

# Arithmetic with timedelta
tomorrow = now + timedelta(days=1)
next_week = now + timedelta(weeks=1)
hour_ago = now - timedelta(hours=1)

# Difference between dates
birthday = date(2000, 5, 15)
today = date.today()
age_days = (today - birthday).days
print(f"Days since birthday: {age_days}")

title: 26. Type Hinting
description:
Type hints (introduced in Python 3.5) allow you to annotate variables and functions with their expected types. Python does not enforce these at runtime — they are primarily for static analysis tools (like mypy) and to improve code readability for developers.

Basic Syntax

Annotate function parameters and return values using : type and -> type.

Common Types from the typing Module

  • List[str] — A list of strings
  • Dict[str, int] — A dict with string keys and int values
  • Optional[str] — Either a string or None
  • Tuple[int, str] — A tuple of specific types
  • Union[int, str] — Either int or str

Note: In Python 3.10+, you can use built-in types directly: list[str], dict[str, int], int | None.

title: 27. Python Virtual Environments
description:
A virtual environment is an isolated Python environment that has its own set of installed packages, separate from the system Python installation. This is crucial for managing dependencies across different projects.

Why Use Virtual Environments?

  • Dependency isolation: Project A needs Flask 2.0, Project B needs Flask 3.0 — no conflict
  • Clean system: No polluting your global Python installation with project-specific packages
  • Reproducibility: Anyone can recreate the exact same environment using requirements.txt

Workflow

  1. Create a virtual environment in the project folder
  2. Activate it (every time you work on the project)
  3. Install packages with pip — they install only in this environment
  4. Save dependencies with pip freeze
  5. Deactivate when done
code:
# ======= Command Line Steps =======

# 1. Create a virtual environment named 'venv'
python -m venv venv

# 2. Activate it:
#    Windows:
venv\Scripts\activate
#    Mac / Linux:
source venv/bin/activate

# Your prompt will change: (venv) C:\...

# 3. Install packages (only in this env)
pip install flask requests numpy

# 4. See what's installed
pip list

# 5. Save dependencies
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# 6. Recreate env on another machine
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 7. Deactivate the environment
deactivate

# Tip: Add 'venv/' to your .gitignore
# so you don't commit the environment folder

title: 28. Pip & Dependencies
description:
pip (Package Installer for Python) is Python's standard package manager. It connects to the PyPI (Python Package Index) — a repository of hundreds of thousands of open-source Python packages.

Key pip Commands

  • pip install package — Install latest version
  • pip install package==1.2.3 — Install specific version
  • pip install package>=1.0 — Install at least version 1.0
  • pip uninstall package — Remove a package
  • pip list — Show all installed packages
  • pip show package — Show details of a specific package
  • pip freeze > requirements.txt — Save all current packages and versions
  • pip install -r requirements.txt — Install from file (reproducible installs)

Popular Packages

requests (HTTP) | flask (web) | numpy (math) | pandas (data) | pillow (images) | pytest (testing)
code:
# === Terminal / Command Line ===

# Install a package
pip install requests

# Install specific version
pip install Flask==3.0.3

# Install minimum version
pip install "numpy>=1.24"

# Upgrade a package
pip install --upgrade flask

# Uninstall
pip uninstall requests

# List installed packages
pip list

# Show package details
pip show flask
# Name: Flask
# Version: 3.0.3
# Location: ...
# Requires: Jinja2, Werkzeug, ...

# Save to requirements.txt
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# Install from requirements.txt
pip install -r requirements.txt

# === requirements.txt example ===
# Flask==3.0.3
# requests==2.31.0
# numpy>=1.24.0

title: 29. Asyncio (Basics)
description:
Asyncio is Python's library for writing asynchronous, concurrent code using the async/await syntax. It's ideal for I/O-bound tasks like web scraping, API calls, or reading/writing files — tasks where your program spends time waiting.

Key Concepts

  • Coroutine — A function defined with async def. It doesn't run immediately when called — it returns a coroutine object
  • await — Pauses the coroutine and gives control back to the event loop until the awaited task is done
  • Event Loop — The heart of asyncio; it manages and runs coroutines concurrently
  • asyncio.gather() — Run multiple coroutines concurrently (truly parallel for I/O tasks)

Async vs Sync

In synchronous code, tasks run one after another. With async, while one task is waiting for I/O, another can run — greatly improving throughput.
code:
import asyncio

# async def defines a coroutine
async def fetch_data(name, delay):
    print(f"{name}: Starting...")
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)   # Simulates network wait
    print(f"{name}: Done!")
    return {"source": name, "data": delay * 100}

# Running one coroutine
async def main():
    result = await fetch_data("API", 1)
    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())

# Running multiple concurrently (faster!)
async def main_concurrent():
    # Both tasks run at the same time
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch_data("Task A", 2),
        fetch_data("Task B", 1),
        fetch_data("Task C", 3),
    )
    # Takes ~3s total, not 2+1+3=6s!
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main_concurrent())

title: 30. Making API Requests
description:
The requests library is the standard way to make HTTP requests in Python (install with pip install requests). It simplifies interacting with web APIs, fetching web pages, and sending data to servers.

HTTP Methods

  • GET — Retrieve data from a server
  • POST — Send data to create a new resource
  • PUT / PATCH — Update existing data
  • DELETE — Delete a resource

Response Object

  • response.status_code — HTTP status (200=OK, 404=Not Found, 500=Server Error)
  • response.json() — Parse JSON body as a Python dict
  • response.text — Raw response body as a string
  • response.headers — Response headers dict

Error Handling

Always check the status code or use response.raise_for_status() to automatically raise an exception for 4xx/5xx errors.
code:
import requests

# GET Request
response = requests.get("https://api.github.com")
if response.status_code == 200:
    data = response.json()
    print(data["current_user_url"])

# GET with query parameters
params = {"q": "python", "sort": "stars"}
r = requests.get("https://api.github.com/search/repositories",
                 params=params)
print(r.json()["total_count"])

# POST Request
payload = {"title": "My Task", "completed": False}
res = requests.post(
    "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos",
    json=payload   # Automatically sets Content-Type: application/json
)
print(res.status_code)  # 201 Created
print(res.json())

# With headers (e.g., Authentication)
headers = {"Authorization": "Bearer your_token_here"}
r = requests.get("https://api.example.com/data",
                 headers=headers)

# Auto-raise exception for bad responses
try:
    r.raise_for_status()
except requests.HTTPError as e:
    print(f"HTTP Error: {e}")
